text: The surgeon establishes pneumoperitoneum using an open technique, followed by sequential insertion of ports under direct vision. The robotic side cart is docked to the patient's left hip, and the robotic arms, starting with the camera arm, are attached to the ports and checked for proper positioning and movement.

The surgeon begins the medial to lateral approach by incising the peritoneum and identifying the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA). The dissection plane is developed close to the back of the IMA, revealing the left ureter and gonadal vessels. The IMA is skeletonized, and Hemolog clips are applied proximally and distally before division. The inferior mesenteric vein is traced to the duodenal-jejunal junction and divided with Hemolog clips, allowing further development of the medial plane into the lesser sac above the pancreas. The lesser sac is visualized, and a swab is placed at the top of the pancreas for identification.

The surgeon releases the colon laterally, connecting it with the previously mobilized medial plane. For splenic flexure mobilization, the R-II is disengaged, while R-I with monopolar scissors and R-III with bipolar forceps are used to divide the gastrocolic ligament. The assistant provides downward counter-traction to aid in mobilization. Upon entering the lesser sac, the swab placed over the pancreas is visualized and retrieved. The splenic flexure mobilization is completed by releasing the lateral attachments of the splenocolic ligament.

An additional eight-millimeter port is inserted in the left iliac fossa for the total mesorectal excision (TME). The surgeon uses articulating monopolar scissors in R-I for precise dissection of the posterior plane, while providing counter-traction with bipolar forceps in R-III. The dissection stays anterior to the presacral fascia to avoid injury to the hypogastric nerves and presacral venous plexus. The interior plane is developed with a peritoneal cut made two cm above the peritoneal reflection, preserving nerve fibers posterior to the seminal vesicles. The lateral pillars are dissected and joined to the anterior plane from the left.

The surgeon meticulously develops the anterior plane anterior to the adenovillous fascia, using traction with R-II and counter-traction with R-III. The left and right sides are extended to the pelvic floor, ensuring not to dissect too widely to avoid nerve damage. The rectal tube is carefully thinned by dividing the adenovillous fascia anteriorly, avoiding diathermy damage. The dissection reaches the pelvic floor with the overlying endopelvic fascia.

The surgeon replaces the right iliac fossa port with a twelve millimeter port to accommodate a roticulating forty-five millimeter laparoscopic stapler. The stapler is used to perform an anterior-posterior transection, with two fire rings ensuring precise cutting. The pelvis is inspected, revealing intact left and right hypogastric nerves, and the smooth, intact total mesorectal excision (TME) package is displayed.
